data.title: As Chicago Thaws In Spring, CME Group Remains A Hot Dividend Idea
data.description:
More Than Just Another Chicago Exchange

As another earnings season is upon us this April, I thought about which stock to do a preview of ahead of its next earnings and CME Group (CME) is one that has consistently been on my watch list since 2024.

This Chicago-based firm "operates contract markets for the trading of futures and options on futures contracts worldwide," according to its Seeking Alpha profile, and its roots lie in the Chicago Mercantile Exchange.

With its next earnings results due out on Apr. 22nd, and having beaten the last three estimates, today's article offers a preview of where this stock has been lately and where it could be going.

As recap, since my buy rating in Dec. 2024, the stock is up around +28%, and is also up since my followup buy ratings in July 2025 and Oct 2025, indicating my bullish view proved correct multiple times.

Some reasons I liked the stock so much was the demand for data analytics solutions, robust trading volumes amidst periods of volatility, and its role as the name behind a major exchange that is such a critical part of modern financial markets.

There You Go Again! Thesis Reaffirms Prior Highly Bullish View

In my updated research today, I determined there is plenty to cheer for with CME and reaffirm a strong buy rating, actually contrarian to the overwhelmingly neutral/hold consensus as of Monday (Wall St, SA analysts, quant system).

This time around, this stock dominated across my entire 8-point review, with bullish or highly bullish scores in each. In particular, strong upside could come from demand for market data and continued trading volumes, the firm's diversified mix, strong margins, and very low leverage. Technical patterns are still resilient, while valuation remains reasonable.

In The Face of Macro Volatility, This Sector Has Proven Its Resilience

When focusing on the big picture, the value of market data and the role of market volatility could both continue benefiting this sector, so I am highly bullish in this section.

What really sticks out from CME's last quarterly deck (pg 3) is a steady growth in their "market data" business, so I took a closer look at whether there could continue to be demand at a macroeconomic level.

Considering that it was McKinsey who wrote in early 2025 that "financial data and markets infrastructure is one of the fastest-growing segments of financial services," I expect the tailwind to not lose steam just yet.

CME's deck also highlighted YoY growth in clearing and transactions fees, so a trend of trading volume growth could also be a tailwind, particularly trading of futures and options.

It seems to me the recent geopolitical and oil shocks since March, and continually changing political messaging from Washington, could actually drive continued volatility and traders betting one way or another. We saw some of that with the tariff wars of 2025, rather than investors retreating under a rock.

For instance, when it comes to options, for instance, the CBOE reported in January:

2025 was the sixth consecutive record year for U.S. listed options. Unprecedented industry participation was driven by strong equity performance, bouts of elevated volatility and increased retail and institutional flow.

Further, CME itself said in its Q4 earnings release that in FY25 they saw a "12% growth in commodities trading," despite all of the media noise about tariffs last year and their economic impact, and in fact Investopedia wrote in February that "the size and diversity of the U.S. economy usually protects it from sharp downturns," citing Ben Harris of the Brookings Institution.

If you asked me personally, I prefer the world to be trading with each other than not, and so I think markets have been tested last year and now, showing considerable resilience.

Competitive Positioning Driven by New Regions, and Impressive Return on Equity

When it comes to CME's competitive positioning in its sector, I gave it a bullish score here due after considering both historic data and qualitative factors.

A new angle to this stock that I did not include in my very first article, is a comparison with 3 similar peers across a few metrics, and I chose peers in the niche of financial exchanges and data: Nasdaq (NDAQ), Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), and S&P Global (SPGI).

From historic peer data, I considered CME's top and bottom-line performance vs peers, and CME fell behind the peer group in this case with a 5-year revenue CAGR of +5.9% but was closer to the top with a return on common equity of +14.5% and a peer-leading net income margin of +62.5%. This could indicate a strong earnings efficiency, which I will explore more in a later section.

I also considered the 10-year price return vs the S&P500, with a goal of achieving alpha, or exceeding the benchmark's return. CME had a respectable run, a +216% return, coming just shy of the benchmark, while its 3 peers did considerably better here, indicating stronger momentum from investors:

While those points paint a quantitative picture based on historic data, from a qualitative angle I would point to some things the firm mentioned in their Q4 investor deck which I think could help their competitive positioning in this sector as well as global penetration of financial markets.

Notable items include "cryptocurrency F&O available to trade 24 hours a day, seven days a week," as well as the launch of CME Securities Clearing in 2026, and the "opening of a Dubai Office fueled by the surging institutional and retail participation in financial markets in the region."

A Proven Revenue Grower Already, Diversified Across Exchanges and Market Data Solutions

Next, I was highly bullish when it comes to company-specific growth factors and here is why.

Understanding what drives company growth this specific niche within the financial sector is good first step, since unlike banks it is not loan growth and also not net client inflows like at asset managers.

In the case of CME, we already said earlier they are driven by trading volumes and demand for market data. In the upcoming Q1 earnings results, I'll be looking for a continuation of the trend below, which is proven revenue growth in the market data segment:

Also, investors and analysts will be looking for a continuation of growth in ADV (average daily volume) in trading across a diverse set of categories, to build upon the growth in FY25 which we saw in the Q4 investor deck (pg. 3):

In fact, January data highlighted in the last investor deck (pg. 8) showed that CME already kicked off 2026 with strong momentum, seeing 55% of trading days in January with over +30MM contracts traded:

Further complementing this growth story is Seeking Alpha data showing expected fwd revenue growth of +6.1%, which further supports a bullish stance. This could continue building on CME being a proven revenue grower each year since 2021, according to income statement data:

A Positive Earnings Outlook Could Keep Attracting Bulls

Moving on to the earnings outlook, I would give this stock another highly bullish score in this section too after considering their margins, cost structure, and consensus forecasts.

Earlier, I highlighted the strong net margin of this firm among peers, and here to add an additional angle I would mention that CME also has the best operating margin of this peer group, at nearly +65%, showing that its core business is doing well at generating core earnings:

Unlike the equity REITs I write about who are burdened with property expenses, or pharma and its heavy R&D costs, I expect CME's business model to be largely impacted by the cost of infrastructure/data/tech since that is core to what they do. In fact, as they continually innovate, I expect more investment in tech and data to support new products/solutions.

Nevertheless, the consensus is highly confident about future EPS growth, giving this stock +13 upward revisions (as of Monday data) and 0 downward, as well as a YoY EPS growth forecast of 4-10% this year and next, which I think is not unrealistic considering this firm's strong margins among peers.

The Balance Sheet Risk Story Is Overwhelmingly an Attractive One

In reviewing balance sheet risk, particularly the D/E vs peers, credit ratings, and asset risk, I would give this stock a bullish score here.

The debt-to-equity for CME is one of the lowest I've seen in stocks I've rated in the last few years, at just 0.13, and the lowest in this peer group which seems to not be over-leveraged to begin with. This certainly moves the risk story into a favorable range for me.

It also indicates the business can fund itself largely from operations and from equity rather than heavy borrowing but also can keep down interest expense in a time when interest rates are elevated.

I would also consider what a major credit rating agency says about CME, and notable is that in February, Fitch affirmed an investment-grade "AA-" rating for CME, saying in its rating note that "the company's leverage levels have historically been conservative relative to those of its more acquisitive peers."

While this firm is not a lender or exposed to the asset risk of loans and mortgages, it does have a significant about of "goodwill" on its balance sheet and so the risk to consider is whether the firm will have to write down goodwill in the future. This is why I was not a strong buy here but only modestly bullish, in consideration of that risk.

A Dividend Stock Winning on Yield, Growth, and Sustainability

As a dividend income investor, I like to include a section on dividends too so after considering yield, growth, and sustainability, I am once again highly bullish and actually will include CME in my dividend ideas for April.

For this type of stock, which is not a high-yield mortgage REIT, it exceeds its sector median with a dividend yield of 3.8%, so this could still be interesting if looking for attractive yields.

On top of that, CME also has a proven dividend growth case too, with a 10-year dividend growth CAGR of +9.17% (as of Monday's data), also beating its sector median considerably and showing it can grow dividend over many years and market cycles.

In terms of sustainability, Seeking Alpha data shows its dividend payout ratio around 44%, so well over half of earnings remain after dividends. Being a company with low leverage, which I showed earlier, this could mean more money to invest in continued growth through innovation, as well as cash for share repurchases to help support the share price. The cashflow statement, for instance, shows the firm doing share repurchases over several recent quarters.

Reasonably Valued Within Peer Group, While Further Upside is Possible

In my valuation and price forecast worksheet below, which uses Seeking Alpha data as inputs, I determined the stock is reasonably valued yet shows further upside potential, so I gave this section a bullish view.

The stock's fwd P/E multiple is mostly in line with the peer group average, so it seems the market expects it to perform in line and not a major outlier, while its price/book value is only slightly undervalued and also not a major outlier. Some may question whether a nearly 25x fwd P/E multiple is justified, but earlier I showed how CME dominates in both operating and net margin, and has strong consensus forecasts.

My price forecast for Dec. 2027 is $317.76, expecting a +6% upside, and supporting a modest bullish view. This is in the same range as the average Wall St rating as of Monday, which forecasts a nearly +4% upside.

Technical Patterns See a Slight Dip, But the Bulls Could Still be Lingering

We've talked a lot about fundamentals, so for some more diversity I considered the technical chart I made below, comparing the share price over 1 year vs the 50-day and 200-day SMA, and based on this I am modestly bullish.

In this chart pattern, there are no clear buy or sell signals such as golden crosses or death crosses. Instead, the share price has been on a bullish trend since the start of the year, mostly trading above both moving averages shown, and recently saw a slight dip but not an extended break in the trend either.

For this reason, I think the bullish trend can still hold up, especially since both moving averages continue to have an upward slope. This technical pattern seems to support, rather than contradict, the otherwise bullish tone of this article.

I expect the market is eager to see what the upcoming Q1 results will say, and I think a key metric will be whether CME continues to meet or beat EPS estimates. A beat could send the price above the 50-day SMA again, and that is what I'm betting on at this juncture.

A Key Risk Could Come From Cyber Attacks on Exchanges and Data

One key risk that is actually sector-specific and ongoing, rather than near-term, is related to the role CME and peers play in not only running major exchanges but also in accurate and reliable market data.

As someone who worked many years in enterprise IT roles, I always think about cyber risk, and this sector in particular appears front of mind when it comes to the risk of attacks, a risk that can only grow if global geopolitical conflicts/wars continue, since targeting a country's major exchanges could certainly wreak havoc on markets but also in investor confidence.

In fact, just this week I read a story in the Financial Times this week highlighting concerns of risks to the financial sector from the latest AI model by Anthropic, and "leading British banks, insurers and exchanges will be warned about the cyber security risks."

Conclusion Reaffirms Prior Strong Buy

To wrap up today's discussion, I continue to be a strong buy on this stock, after considering several fundamental factors but also technical ones.

In addition, CME is one of my dividend ideas this month, so I would suggest to dividend income investors to include it in your watchlist for further research/analysis, but also can be a growth idea as well particularly driven by its focus on new products/solutions and global growth too.

data.title: Why Apple Still Wins Here
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Apple (AAPL) is entering Q2 FY2026 with a rare degree of momentum, having seen increased estimates, high iPhone sales and even stronger-than-expected demand in China amid a challenging market environment. With 27 upgrades to the company's financial outlook on both revenues and EPS, it's clear that sentiments have changed but the actual story here is margin protection.

While DRAM prices soar up to 90% in the market, Apple counteracts the headwinds through aggressive pricing, operational efficiencies and vendor power. While AAPL's performance is muted since my last coverage, I stay bullish because its unmatched pricing power, scale and cash flow allow it to turn cost pressures into higher-margin growth rather than headwinds.

Topline Forecasts and Cash Operations Counteract 90% Memory Cost Escalations

I see a long-term buy opportunity, as Apple prepares to report Q2 results on April 30. For Q2, consensus estimates have an EPS of $1.94 (+17.7% YoY growth) and topline of $109.20 billion with 14.52% YoY increase. Following the trend, I see, over the last 3-months, Apple received 27 EPS increase estimates and 1 decrease estimate.

Similarly, topline estimates have 27 increase estimates and 1 decrease estimate. Moreover, I see positive trends in production and sales. Like 52 million iPhone builds for the Q2 and this volume points to a 12% YoY increase. With that, as per Counterpoint Research, there is a 23% YoY surge in iPhone sales in China during the first 9-weeks of CY26. This sales volume is against a 4% decline in the China smartphone market.

Apple also launched new products in March like the MacBook Neo at a $599 entry price and the iPhone 17e at $599. Further, Apple refreshed the iPad Air, MacBook Air, and MacBook Pro lines that incorporate higher entry prices on MacBooks. For instance, the 13-inch MacBook Air starts at $1,099 and the 14-inch MacBook Pro starts at $2,199. These price increases may offset memory component cost inflation as DRAM prices increased 80%-90% QoQ in Q1-CY26.

Looking at the Street, as per Morgan Stanley, Q2 App Store growth forecast is at 8% YoY and Services growth forecast is at 13.5% YoY. Similarly, UBS points to a 7% increase in App Store topline for Q1-CY26 and differences exist across geographies. Like, the China App Store topline increased 4% YoY, but the US App Store topline declined 2%.

The main indicator is the Lead times for Mac computers increased in February as Mac Studio lead times reached 27 days and MacBook Pro M4 lead times reached 23 days. Mainly, Apple minimizes supply chain cost increases impact through scale and pricing power. In my opinion, how Apple negotiates semiconductor procurement contracts may decide Q2 two earnings outcomes/profitability. Apple already has solid cash growth (+24.4% in Q1) and operational cash flow ($135.47 billion, TTM) to face industry headwinds (like a memory component crisis).

Amid DRAM prices surge, Memory parts account for 30% of notebook materials. Apple leverages scale to secure component contracts with suppliers. Furthermore, Apple transfers cost escalations to end users as Apple raised prices across its Mac lineup while maintaining strong demand.

Thus, Apple may maintain Q1 profit margins through this pricing action. As Apple launched the MacBook Neo, CY26 projection points MacBook Neo shipments between 4.5 million -5 million units this year. This laptop has the A18 Pro processor and targets the education market against Google Chromebooks and Microsoft Surface laptops. On the iPhone side, Apple has a 21% smartphone market share and Q1 shipments grew 5% YoY against Industry smartphone shipments decline of 6% YoY.

Looking forward, Apple uses hardware sales to funnel users into services as the Services segment has solid user engagement. Spend per month per user increased to $13 from $9. Here, in detail, a survey pointed out that 63% of users subscribe to AppleCare/Apple One, Apple Pay usage increased to 40%, Apple TV adoption reached 54%, and Apple Music usage hit 61%.

Now, Apple plans to deploy AI enhancements by integrating Gemini and Claude models with updates to the Siri assistant. Apple tests a Gemini application for Mac computers and incorporates the M5 processor into MacBook models. So? The M5 processor scales up to 40 cores and improves AI compute performance by four times. With that, Apple builds a moat through this hardware and software integration. Now, the hardware ecosystem locks consumers into the ecosystem and this lock-in guarantees subscription service topline, in my view.

As the service topline has margin expansion, the combination of hardware sales and service subscriptions boosts long-term financial performance. This protects Apple’s financials from macro volatility as cash volumes may handle supply chain disruptions. How? For instance, Apple possesses a 20% stake in Globalstar as it invested $400 million in Globalstar equity. Apple paid $1.1 billion for Globalstar infrastructure and operates the Emergency SOS feature using Globalstar’s satellites. Amazon is considering acquiring Globalstar, but Apple secures infra access despite Amazon’s moves. This is how Apple protects consumer features through the use of cash.

Apple continues investing in US manufacturing and Apple pledged $600 billion over 4-years to production inside the US. For this, Apple partners with TSMC in Arizona, Amkor Technology, Cirrus Logic, and Bosch. These deals mean that Apple builds a supply chain inside the US as the US government scrutinizes manufacturing outside the US. Apple’s cash is reducing geopolitical risks through US investments. With that, Apple shifts 55 million iPhone units to India assembly lines. I believe the continuous smart use of cash may support Apple to diversify assembly geography and reduce downside risk to the stock.

Risks To Growth Estimates Amid Fines Issues And High P/E Premium

On the downside, I think, Apple continues to endure antitrust actions across geographies. As the European Commission fined Apple €1.84 billion in March 2024 and this fine punished Apple for music streaming distribution rules. Now, The European Commission fined Apple €500 million in April 2025. The fine punished Apple for anti-steering rules and these fines amount to €2.34 billion.

The European broadcasters advocate that there should be antitrust regulation of Apple TV, while Apple can be termed as a gatekeeper by the Digital Markets Act. The broadcasters advocate regulation of smart TV operating systems, meaning Apple will remain under pressure regarding its topline in Europe. The topline of Apple is also likely to face pressure in China.

The Chinese government scrutinizes the App Store ecosystem and Apple has already reduced commission rates in China (cut the commission rate to 25% from 30%). Here, Apple cut the commission rate for the Small Business Program to 12% from 15%. This reduction directly limits service topline flow in China. I believe China represents a volume market for Apple and a reduction in service topline in China may harm its financial performance through margin compression.

I see that subsidies in India already expired on March 31. This loss of subsidies can hurt assembly cost structures in upcoming quarters. With that, Apple imports semiconductors from Taiwan and South Korea. In my view, this supply chain is also under risk of disruptions in East Asia. This is bad for AAPL as it makes the stock subject to price correction amid a 40% premium on P/E and 105% premium on PEG non-GAAP over the sector median.

Takeaway

As we move into earnings season, Apple's edge is in its execution, pricing leverage, strong demand and Services revenue growth will more than overcome any cost headwinds.

data.title: Fair Isaac Corporation Has Finally Dropped Enough For An Upgrade
data.description:
Back in August of 2025, I decided to take a fresh look at shares of Fair Isaac Corporation (FICO). Honestly, in many respects, it's the kind of company that I love. It is a rapidly growing cash cow of a business that should do well in the long run. But back then, I remained unhappy about how expensive the stock was. In fact, I even went so far as to reaffirm it as a Sell candidate. Since then, shares have fallen 26.8%, while the S&P 500 is up 7.9%. But that's nothing. The stock is actually down 44.9% since I first rated it a Sell back in April of that year. The market, meanwhile, is up 33.5%.

Interestingly, this downside can be attributed entirely to the fact that the stock was overpriced. After all, the business continued to post attractive growth year over year all the way up through the first quarter of 2026. Now, the stock is still rather expensive on an absolute basis. But compared to where it has been in the past, it is priced low enough in my view to justify upgrading it to a Hold.

Hopefully, as the new data comes in, I will be given a reason to upgrade it even further. I would love little more than to do that. The good news is that, in the coming days, management will be announcing results for the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year on or about April 27th. The current expectation is that revenue and profitability will rise materially. And if we have some insight into what the future might look like, and if that indicates an attractive valuation on a forward basis, upgrading the stock further could make sense.

Checking in on Fair Isaac Corporation

Operationally speaking, Fair Isaac Corporation strikes me as a fascinating business. The largest chunk of the firm, as measured by profitability, happens to be its Scores segment, which is famous for its FICO Score that has served as one of the three largest credit ratings in the U.S. In fact, management has described its FICO Score as the U.S. industry standard measure of consumer credit risk for more than 35 years now. It's used by more than 90% of U.S. lenders on a B2B basis, including when it comes to considering approvals for mortgages, auto loans, and credit cards. And on the consumer side, it is used for things like licensing, open access, and more.

Although it's not as relevant when it comes to profitability, the Software segment of the company is incredibly important from a sales standpoint. Even though it was responsible for just 17.9% of segment profits in the most recent quarter, it generated 40.5% of the company's revenue. This part of the company provides customers with analytics and digital decisioning technologies that help businesses to automate, improve, and connect decisions across their enterprise. It operates in more than 80 countries across the globe, meaning that it truly is an integral part of the global economy.

The most recent data that we have regarding the business covers through the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company amounted to $512 million. That represents an increase of 16.4% compared to the $440 million that the business reported a year earlier. As revenue for the company expanded, profitability improved also. Net income rose from $152.5 million to $158.4 million. This is not to say that every profitability metric for the business improved. Operating cash flow, as an example, dropped from $194 million to $174.1 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $195.2 million to $219.2 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business expanded from $183.1 million to $238.1 million.

The chart above shows financial results for 2025 in its entirety compared to 2024. This is proof that the first quarter on its own was not a blip on the radar. In fact, it is part of a broader trend that the company has experienced. Most of the expansion that the company has enjoyed as of late can be chalked up to its Scores business. For the first quarter, for instance, revenue was $304.5 million. That's 29.2% higher than the $235.7 million reported the previous year. Management claimed that most of this revenue growth, about $66.2 million of the $68.9 million, can be attributed to the B2B side of things, as the company was able to charge higher prices and enjoyed a rise in volume associated with mortgage originations.

At first glance, this might seem a bit peculiar. After all, home-buying activity has taken a hit in recent years because of inflationary pressures and weakening economic conditions. However, the Federal Reserve has been slashing interest rates, even if those rate cuts have come at a slow pace. And as interest rates come down, the ability to buy homes increases. The chart below, as an example, shows that, through July of last year, we had seen the volume of loans originated rise for quite some time. Admittedly, it was showing signs of weakening. But it sure as heck beats what we saw the years before that. I wish I could say that we had more updated data. However, the results provided by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau are meaningfully delayed.

As revenue for this segment increases, profitability rises also. After all, this is a high-margin software and services company. Those do have a tendency to do quite well. Operating profits rose from $203.8 million to $267.9 million. This also translated to a rise in profit margin from 86.47% to 87.98%. For 2025 as a whole, the $1.03 billion in profits generated by the segment translated to a profit margin of 87.81%. That's actually down from the 88.44% reported the previous year. So what this seems to indicate is that margins are now expanding after having a bit of a down year in 2025.

By comparison, the Software side of the business is not doing as well. Revenue barely budged, climbing from $204.3 million to $207.4 million. The firm benefited in large part from professional services that it offers, which grew from $18.3 million to $19.2 million. However, there was a bit of an increase when it came to on-premises and SaaS software revenue, with an increase from $186 million to $188.2 million. Management continues to tout their growth for its platform products. Typically, a rise in revenue here would translate to higher profits. But in this case, we actually saw a decline in profitability from $60.7 million to $58.6 million. Even though the company benefited from higher revenue, it was negatively impacted by a rise in third-party data center hosting expenses and a reduction in sales associated with its higher-margin software.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year, management expects further expansion. They anticipate that revenue will rise from $1.99 billion last year to about $2.35 billion this year. The expectation for net profits would be $795 million, while management anticipates adjusted net income of $907 million. I actually didn't really focus on adjusted net profits earlier in this article solely because I am not a fan of companies adding stock-based compensation back into the mix. If you want to do that, you may as well just look at cash flow. Speaking of which, if other profitability metrics rise at the same rate that net income should, we would get adjusted operating cash flow for this year of $972.4 million and EBITDA totaling $1.16 billion.

The chart above shows how the company is priced using these metrics. For most businesses, this would indicate that the stock needs to be rated in a bearish manner. After all, as a value investor, I cannot usually stand companies that are trading at ridiculously high multiples. Having said that, I am more forgiving when it comes to high-quality cash cows that are growing nicely. And as I demonstrated already throughout this article, Fair Isaac Corporation certainly fits that description.

Furthermore, compared to what the company has traded at in the past, it's actually priced at levels that are reasonable. The chart below illustrates precisely what I mean here. Keep in mind that there are slight differences in how that chart depicts the trading multiple of the company compared to how I do. So there is not a perfect match between the estimates in my aforementioned chart above and this chart below.

At the start of this article, I mentioned that management will be announcing financial results for the second quarter of the 2026 fiscal year later this month. Management has not actually released an earnings date yet. But most likely it will be on or around April 27th. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue will come in at $623.7 million. That would represent an increase of 25.1% compared to the $498.7 million that the business reported a year earlier. They also believe that earnings per share will climb from $6.59 to $8.96. Without factoring in the prospect of share buybacks, this would mean a rise in net income from $162.6 million to $214.7 million.

It is likely that overall net profits will be a bit lower considering that management does have a history of buying back shares. In fact, during the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, the company spent $163 million to repurchase 95,000 shares. The table also shows certain cash flow metrics for the second quarter of 2025. If revenue and profits increase, it is highly likely that these cash flow metrics will expand as well.

Takeaway

The way I see it, things are going quite well for Fair Isaac Corporation. The stock has been punished over the last several months now because of how expensive it is. But now, I would say that the time is ripe for an upgrade. I'm not quite ready to do a double upgrade and push the stock up to a Buy just yet. But I do think that rating it a Hold is appropriate.

data.title: SCZ ETF: A Weaker Option Within A Struggling Asset Class
data.description:
SCZ Overview

The iShares MSCI EAFE Small-Cap ETF (SCZ) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (a.k.a an ETF) that invests in small-cap stocks from developed markets outside the United States and Canada. BlackRock launched the fund in December 2007, and it has grown to $14Bn in net assets — making it a legit vehicle in the foreign small/mid-cap blend category by AUM. The fund’s benchmark is the MSCI EAFE Small Cap Index, and as a full-replication passive fund, BlackRock simply holds a proportionate share of the index constituents, rebalancing periodically in line with index changes.

After years of sluggish results, SCZ did well in 2025, but the fund has been stuck in limbo amidst the middle east war and an issue I've had with them is cost — and in the passive ETF space, cost is a huge part of the game.

SCZ’s benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Small Cap Index, is a credible and well-constructed benchmark covering the small-cap segment of developed markets in Europe and Asia. Passive investing means the fund managers are not making active bets, they are tracking the index. But when a passive fund charges 0.40% to do something its competitors do just as well for 0.07% or 0.11%, I wonder what you are actually paying for. I rate this fund a sell - both due to the management of the fund and given that Europe and Asia get oil from Iran - which is currently blocked by both Iran and the U.S.

Fund Breakdown

SCZ was launched on December 10, 2007, by BlackRock’s iShares. The fund currently holds 2,000+ stocks, making it one of the broadest portfolios in the category by holding count. This is a genuine differentiator — normally, actively managed foreign small-cap funds often hold 40 to 80 names, and even many passive peers hold fewer constituents. With the top 10 positions accounting for just 2.92% of total assets, no single company or sector event is going to materially impair the NAV. That said, the fund has struggled versus other cheaper indexes, and is among the worst performers of its peer set year-to-date.

From a liquidity standpoint, SCZ is well-suited to institutional and retail investors alike, averaging about 1.27MM shares traded per day worth over $80MM. But for the vast majority of individual investors, funds like Vanguard FTSE All-World ex-US Small-Cap Index ETF (VSS) and Schwab International Small-Cap Equity ETF (SCHC) are both sufficiently liquid that SCZ’s liquidity premium does not justify the fee premium.

The fund employs a 0.40% expense ratio. For context, this is the same fee charged by many actively managed ETFs in the space, which at least have the justification of paying for a portfolio manager’s judgement. When I compare it to VSS at 0.07% or SCHC at 0.11%, SCZ costs an investor roughly 29 to 33 basis points per year more, every year, for a nearly identical outcome. On a $100,000 investment compounded over 10 years, that fee gap represents roughly $3,000 to $3,500 in fees.

Q2 Outlook

While I wanted to be bullish on small caps heading into 2026 - higher energy costs and an unclear tariff picture make me want to reduce exposure. While the valuation gap between U.S. and international equities has widened to historically extreme levels over the past decade, small-cap equities in developed international markets carry a higher domestic revenue exposure than their large-cap peers, which means a struggling Europe and higher gas prices >$2/L will directly hit this fund more than a small-cap U.S. fund.

And the Q1 results were a reminder that SCZ is not always the sharpest tool even within its own category. Whether that is noise or a sign that VSS and SCHC’s slightly different index construction is producing better results, it reinforces my view that SCZ is not even necessarily the best passive option in the space, let alone the best overall option.

Fund Portfolio & Risk Profile

Reviewing SCZ’s portfolio as of December 31, 2025, the sector and geographic breakdown reflect what you would expect from a broad index of developed international small caps. Industrials lead at 24% of the fund, followed by Financials (13%), and Materials (12%). Geographically, Japan is the dominant weight at 36% of the fund, followed by the United Kingdom (12%), and Australia (11%). The Japan concentration is notable and means that SCZ investors are implicitly making a significant bet on the Japanese macro story, including the Bank of Japan’s gradual monetary normalization and whether the corporate governance reform wave translates into sustained earnings improvement.

In comparison to peers like VSS, SCHC, and ISCF, they are relatively similar; however these funds have exposure to Canada and more importantly, energy stocks. These are a direct hedge and these global oil companies should do well as private/state run enterprises in the middle east struggle with the Strait of Hormuz closed. This is a big miss for the fund and investors should review that when thinking of international small-cap exposure; especially since small-cap oil is usually unhedged and reap full benefits of prolonged high prices.

Key Risks

From a risk perspective, the fund has an beta of 1.04. The beta above 1 reflects the higher volatility though it is a portfolio diversification characteristic, not necessarily a sign that the fund is higher-risk in isolation. Additionally, the fund's 0.59 Sharpe ratio is underwhelming, as risk adjusted returns for other similar funds have been approaching 1, which is more palatable for investors.

The most significant fund-level risk is one I’ve already flagged: the fee. In a passive indexing context, paying 0.40% when comparable alternatives exist at 0.07% is a structural drag on compounded returns that never goes away. Every year you hold SCZ over VSS, you are handing back roughly 33 basis points to BlackRock for no incremental benefit.

Beyond fees, currency is the primary market-level risk. SCZ holds stocks denominated in yen, pounds, euros, Australian dollars, and Swedish krona, and USD-based investors bear this exposure in full without any hedging - especially given the war context. A meaningful U.S. dollar strengthening — whether from tariff escalation pr a hawkish Federal Reserve pivot — would compress the fund’s USD-denominated returns even if underlying foreign stock prices were flat. This is a known and accepted feature of unhedged international ETFs, but it is a real source of short-term volatility.

Japan’s 36% weighting is also a concentration risk that deserves mention. Any yen appreciation, market volatility, or disappointment in Japan’s corporate governance reform trajectory could have an outsized impact on SCZ relative to peers with a different geographic mix. And on the macro side, tariff uncertainty and European fiscal headwinds are also live risks for an asset class that is only just recovering its footing after years of underperformance.

Bottom Line

The bottom line is that new risks have emerged for the sector, and I don't love the SCZ portfolio construction. While the portfolio is well-diversified across, the fund is liquid, and the Morningstar Bronze rating provides some external validation of the fund’s quality, paying 0.40% for a passive index fund in 2026 is simply unnecessary. If you want international small-cap exposure — and there are good reasons to want it right now — there are more efficient vehicles to get it. For existing SCZ holders with significant embedded capital gains, holding can make sense and I am not suggesting you trigger a tax event to save 33 basis points annually. But for new money entering this asset class, I would not be starting with SCZ.

data.title: CoreWeave: The Best Way To Play The Data Center Boom
data.description:
We’re at a crossroads in the stock market right now, with the S&P 500 largely shaking off the initial woes after conflict in Iran began brewing. The question for investors now is: how can we best position ourselves for a snapback to a risk-on environment?

Broadly speaking, I believe companies with AI tailwinds continue to be a great way to play a potential bull market opening. CoreWeave (CRWV), one of the leading cloud data center providers, is one of the most compelling infrastructure plays in the market, especially after a recent spate of high profile deals.

With very few exceptions, I believe the infrastructure trade, namely in overbought chip stocks, is too expensive especially as their revenue relies on peak demand for under supplied datacenter components (particularly memory). But in my view, CoreWeave stands to benefit through the long haul as the company signs on multi-year, recurring revenue deals at increasingly favorable terms. I’m initiating the stock at a buy rating.

The roadmap to substantial profitability

First, we have to state the obvious. Buying CoreWeave today requires a leap of faith on the company’s future roadmap, as its current revenue and earnings are thin relative to its potential. So the key question here is: where is CoreWeave going?

To understand that, we first dig into the basics of what CoreWeave does and why it’s necessary. CoreWeave, to me, is a mere extension of the cloud movement (though stock market darlings are now careful to avoid any association with the terms “cloud” and “SaaS” since they no longer inspire the buzzy confidence of the past).

Simply put, while companies like Meta (META) and OpenAI are spending billions on building out their own datacenter infrastructure, they also need the flexibility offered by off-premises capacity providers. As usage scales up and down, they need to be able to access “rented” servers to be able to serve spikes in usage and not constrain all compute resources to singular data centers. CoreWeave’s service, in terms of intent and effect, is no different than the SaaS/cloud migration as companies moved more workloads to cloud-based apps and servers rather than hosting everything on-premises.

Why new infrastructure such as that provided by CoreWeave is necessary, however, is that AI workloads are fundamentally more complex and require specialized compute to run. The same resources of the past won’t work, which is what is requiring new datacenter buildouts:

Now, to illustrate where CoreWeave is headed. In the medium term, the company expects to exit the current year (FY26) at a $17-$19 billion ARR run rate. By FY27, it’s expecting further +67% growth to over $30 billion in ARR.

In terms of profitability and unit economics, the company is targeting to hit 25-30% adjusted operating margins. This means that against the company’s medium term expectations of ~$30 billion in annual revenue, it is aiming to profit ~$7.5-$9.0 billion in annual pretax profit. Note as well that the company’s definition of adjusted operating profit includes depreciation and amortization of its largest expense (data center building costs, which is billed to capex) - so its adjusted EBITDA is actually much higher (the company just exited FY25 at a 60% adjusted EBITDA margin).

To me, this $7.5-$9.0 billion in medium/long term annual profit is a good, rough size anchor to evaluate the company at its current ~$60 billion market cap.

Of course, this scale isn’t exactly the same as a near-term guidance figure that the company already has line of sight to. The question of whether CoreWeave can achieve these targets boils down to three elements for me:

  • Does the company have the demand profile to achieve this run rate?
  • Are the unit economics supportive of this target?
  • Is the financing available and at attractive terms?

We’ll examine these items in turn, but cutting to the chase; I find an overwhelming "Yes" to all of these elements, hence my buy rating on the stock.

Demand profile is affirmed via huge backlog growth

Let’s first discuss the company’s demand profile. Of all the question marks on CoreWeave’s roadmap, to me this is the one that CoreWeave has most obviously under its belt.

First, a word of CoreWeave’s revenue recognition. Like most recurring revenue deals, CoreWeave recognizes revenue ratably over time, based on data and power consumption. So while the company’s near term revenue growth (greater than 2x y/y in the most recent quarter) is helpful context, it’s really the backlog and the new deal activity that we’re watching.

As of December, CoreWeave reported a staggering $66.8 billion in backlog. Backlog grew at 342% y/y, and needless to say appetite for compute capacity continues to be voracious.

This backlog profile is largely short term weighted for now. 42% of this backlog will be recognized in the next 24 months (that is, across FY26-FY27), or $28.1 billion. We don’t know the exact linearity within this backlog, but taken ratably this is $14 billion per year across the next two years. We find then that CoreWeave has a great line of sight to its $17-$19 billion exiting FY26 ARR target (its target for actual revenue recognition in FY26 is only $12-$13 billion).

This Q4 backlog snapshot, of course, will be supplemented by new deals along the way. CoreWeave's stock has been rallying recently on two major announcements. In April, Meta announced an expanded multi-year deal with CoreWeave, committing to a whopping $21 billion of spending through 2032.

Similarly, and more recently, Anthropic also announced a wide-spanning, long range deal with CoreWeave, though with no dollar commitments attached to the press release. To me, these marquee deals help to signal the notion that CoreWeave’s long term targets aren’t just a pie in the sky target, but realistic goals that are backed by voracious appetite for data center capacity across hyperscalers (alongside the plethora of startups who need compute resources, for whom rental is the only option - but who aren’t large enough to receive their own press releases).

Unit economics point to healthy margins at scale

We next turn to the more crucial topic of whether CoreWeave can be a profitable business. The true profit here will of course come when CoreWeave is at scale, but for now two charts illustrate the appeal of its business model.

The chart below showcases the typical P&L impact of a typical customer contract, which assumes the capex required upfront can be built out.

There are two key insights to be gleaned from this chart. The first is that, in a typical contract, it only takes roughly 45 days for CoreWeave to provision compute resources to a client. Cost of revenue: which includes all direct costs related to running the data centers, ranging from lease expenses to personnel and electricity, is billed ratably throughout the life of the contract. Meanwhile, depreciation and amortization (of the capex required upfront) begins in full on month 2. Beginning in day 45, 100% of monthly revenue from the commitment begins to be recognized from backlog. The company expects, at scale, to deliver 25% contribution margins on these contracts, which is in line with CoreWeave’s expected 25-30% long term adjusted operating profits.

But of course, this ignores a critical component: CoreWeave must finance its data centers in advance, while the payout over these deals stretches out over a longer arc of time.

The chart below takes more of a cash flow approach to the unit economics, rather than P&L/profit metrics. This illustrates again the same typical 5-year deal, with no customer prepayment of the recurring revenue over that timeframe:

Based on the company’s forecasted model, the company expects to spend ~25% of this revenue on data center running expense. 10% of the capex is funded from the company’s coffers, and 49% of the revenue goes to debt service. These deals generate 16% in free cash flow left over to the company, which created room to finance capex for the next deal.

Financing is becoming more attractive

This now brings us full circle. Viewed from this lens, CoreWeave isn’t really a technology company per se, whose success depends on innovation and R&D: at its heart, CoreWeave is really a finance company, whose primary value-add for its clients is the ability to finance expense data center buildouts and provide a managed solution for compute resources.

CoreWeave’s innovation is financial. On March 31, the company announced a new financing structure that it calls the DDTL 4.0 (delayed draw term loan). This $8.5 billion debt financing, which directly finances a major client’s buildout (we expect a hyperscaler like Meta), is done at the most favorable interest rate terms in the company’s history, with a floating rate component set at SOFR +225bps and a fixed component at 5.9%.

The key element to note about the company’s financing structure is that CoreWeave’s lenders base the company’s financing and interest rates based on the credit profile of the underlying buyer. To me, this correctly captures the risk framework that software investors are concerned about right now (the market has backed away from Oracle (ORCL), for example, because a massive chunk of its RPO is predicated on OpenAI’s ability to stay in business and honor its backlog). The underlying customer is the risk variable; CoreWeave’s unit economics and ability to service its debt, assuming its customers don’t flake, isn’t the variable in question.

As time passes, we expect CoreWeave to be able to deploy this structure in more of its debt financings. The company has already been reducing its average cost of debt, down to a weighted average 9.0% as of the end of FY25.

Today at the time of writing, SOFR sits at 3.61%, so SOFR+225 reflects a 5.86% interest rate, which is very close to the 5.90% fixed rate portion of this $8.5 billion DDTL facility. We expect that with the combination of more pass-through facilities utilizing underlying customer credit profiles, plus declining interest rates which will push the variable portion of debt down, the company’s cost of debt will continue to decrease. (A word of caution: the unit economics waterfalls we reviewed in the previous section already assumed a step-down to 6.5% average interest costs, slightly above where the most recent financing is at but still well below the company’s current 9% cost of debt capital as of its Q4 balance sheet).

Valuation, risks and key takeaways

When it comes to valuing CoreWeave, we note that near term multiples are less meaningful since so much of the business is future driven, and the company still hasn’t achieved the recurring revenue scale yet that is already implied by its backlog.

A typical EV/EBITDA multiple doesn't tell the whole story for CoreWeave, since it excludes the critical upfront capex component required for growth. Nevertheless, the comp set I find to be appropriate for CoreWeave are other datacenter suppliers, primarily of chips and memory components. In this regard, I’d rather pay a ~10x near term adjusted EBITDA multiple for CoreWeave rather than ~25x for a chip supplier like Western Digital (WDC). Also shown below are direct "neocloud" peers like Iren (IREN) and Nebius (NBIS), which have the same business model as CoreWeave.

CoreWeave’s revenue profile is evergreen. It is building up a backlog of recurring revenue contracts. Meanwhile, I view chip suppliers to be at the peak of their demand cycles right now, fueled by purchases by the likes of CoreWeave itself plus other hyperscalers. When the capex boom fades, so will chip sales - whereas CoreWeave will benefit from its backlog and contracts for years to come.

Of course, it goes without saying that there is a long list of risks we need to be mindful of in this name. To me, the biggest two headline risks are:

  • Sustainability of AI companies- Just like Oracle, CoreWeave will rely on companies like Anthropic to stay viable in order to see through to the end of their contracts and backlog. CoreWeave has not officially reported any customer concentration metrics.
  • Appetite for financing- CoreWeave’s ability to keep growing and servicing its backlog hinges almost entirely on its ability to raise financing for its data center buildouts, and at favorable terms that utilize its customers’ credit profiles. With the recent spill out in private credit markets, we could be entering into a more cautious period on AI financing.

That said, I see far more positive catalysts for this stock, especially since the recent slew of high profile deals adding to backlog plus the securing of financing to support these deals suggests that CoreWeave’s bullish view on its growth trajectory and target unit economics is playing out.

Stay long here and ride the upward momentum in this stock.

data.title: The Market Doesn't Seem To Care About The Naval Blockade
data.description:
Moving from “imminent destruction” to a two-week ceasefire, and then escalating to a naval blockade this week, Iranian watchers are definitely having a handful, as President Trump puts them in a spot again.

But trying to analyze the rhetoric from the Trump Administration is simply just too conflating for my liking.

Let's be honest here. President Trump is a politician; he has his midterms coming up in November.

The Republican Party is under pressure to perform, and there could be signs that the Democrats might be moving in to retake the House.

Media narratives are aplenty, but they all have their inherent bias, just to be brutally honest.

For us, I think what we truly want to know is whether things are coming off the worst point in the conflict, and what the way forward is for the U.S. and Iran as we approach this delicate period within the two week ceasefire timeframe.

However, while you contemplate that eventuality, I believe you should know that President Trump also has the option of extending the deadline.

If you've been following the developments closely, you should know that this is nothing new. It is part of what he calls the art of the deal, right?

I know some of you may want to pore deeply into how the blockade will work out for the U.S.

The fact is nobody knows exactly. Sure, we can broadly discuss them here, and then I will inform you why the market hasn't really cared as we go into the print later.

So on to the Navy blockade in the Strait of Hormuz.

Does the U.S. have enough assets to fully execute the blockade? Yes and no, right?

It is ambivalent, and I don't think anyone can give you a direct and definitive answer as to whether it will be a full blockage on the ground, or a partial one, isn't it?

That obviously has ramifications, and implications on how successful or how determined the U.S. is willing to be to shut off the oil artery of the Iranian regime.

James Stavridis provides us some helpful nuggets of information when trying to frame our thoughts around this conundrum. Drawing on his experience as the supreme allied commander of NATO, he used to participate in what was then called a “naval quarantine.”

In his understanding, “a blockade means, quite simply, nothing goes in or out.” But, we already know that the U.S. isn't quite looking at such a draconian measure on all vessels, right?

So, this measure is obviously targeted at Iran, and some argue potentially also at China too.

China has been the biggest buyer of Iranian oil for sure. And because of that, there is a possibility that China might want to intervene with more urgency, and press the Iranians to return to the negotiation table.

We already know that China has already lost supplies from the other parts of the Middle East that have been shut off due to Iranian control of the vital conduit.

And there were reports indicating that China "pressed" Iran to "accept" the temporal ceasefire with the U.S., which preceded the weekend negotiations.

At this point, I think odds about whether China will want to intervene more decisively in a war that the U.S. has started remain unlikely.

And, they might also be waiting and seeing how the naval blockade does unfold, and whether the U.S. might even “allow” China-based vessels or some Iranian oil tankers (bound for China) to make their way out of Hormuz?

Also, China does have strategic oil reserves, yeah, and exceeds a billion barrels, and can last them for two months while only "depleting its emergency reserves by about 10%." So, expecting China to intervene more forcefully is still a large unknown for now, although not impossible.

We know that President Trump is scheduled for a visit to China in May, and it does seem like the Trump administration might want to have the conflict wound down by then, at least the most intense phase of it, before the president makes his way to Beijing.

So, who knows, Trump might even extend an olive branch before the visit to Beijing by letting some oil tankers through (partial blockade), and that's not an impossible proposition, right?

Because we all know from a general study of history that a full blockade helps no one in the long run, and is not sustainable.

Plus, the Strait of Hormuz isn't just about oil, and gas, yeah?

There's a whole host of commodities and minerals coming through that waterway.

Thus, the world will feel immense pain if there is really going to be an extended blockade or anything like that.

The White House isn't dumb, right? They know it represents an escalation against Iran, and it could very well be a stratagem to bring them back to the table quickly, but aligning them closer to the U.S. terms potentially.

It has also been reported that both parties are planning for a subsequent ceasefire negotiation between the U.S. and Iran, although no firm dates have been understood just yet.

So, I believe Iran and Trump know that while the former has an extremely high threshold of pain to endure tremendous suffering, Trump's clock to the midterms is winding down, as we speak.

If such pain were to be felt by Americans, since energy and commodities pricing is global (and that includes fertilizers, yeah, what about our farmers?), the response at the upcoming midterms might not be nice.

The recent Hungary election that ousted the outgoing PM Viktor Orbán offers us a timely reminder not to take the popular vote for granted.

Even “Dr. Doom” Nouriel Roubini doesn't concur that America could win against Iran over time with the naval blockade, given the propensity of the regime to endure deep suffering, including that of its citizens.

Hence, even he agrees (but seemingly reluctantly from the way I see it), that the U.S. has decided to go for a “half-escalation” measure to find a way out of this war.

Possible? Yes, possible.

Just when we thought all hope was lost, the position of not having any nuclear enrichment at all appears to be shifting from the U.S. perspective.

The Wall Street Journal reported that Vice President JD Vance offered a “20 year moratorium” on uranium enrichment, of which Iran was keen to offer only five.

I guess this we will continue to see the back and forth on this matter between both parties in the coming days or weeks.

Hopefully shorter, but there appears to be some notable breakthrough from the extreme positions held by Iran and the U.S. previously, no?

Perhaps, this could be the “flexibility” that the Trump administration is willing to extend to the Iranian regime to end this war.

Because it would be a travesty if nothing is being done to Iran's nuclear ambitions linked to this war, right?

If anything, I believe the Iranian regime will almost definitely press harder and push toward uranium enrichment as the ultimate form of deterrence, as they reckon that they haven't been a match for the U.S. in terms of conventional military might.

While their deep fortitude and willingness to suffer may have seen them through this time, they might just reckon that, short of possessing nuclear weapon capabilities, it might never keep them safe from a subsequent attack by the U.S. and Israel.

And the words from North Korean leader Kim Jong Un will ring very loudly in the Iranian leaders' ears.

While these don't remove the long-term threat and realities that the geopolitical order is clearly fracturing and breaking apart, the market could still re-rate itself credibly, right?

Guess what? While we ruminate over whether there's going to be a deal or no deal, the Nasdaq (QQQ, NDX) has climbed back up toward the highs before the war!

The market is looking past these befuddlements and focusing on what matters: valuations and earnings.

And I thought the commentary by Morgan Stanley strategist Mike Wilson couldn't have more aptly captured the signals flashing right before us right now.

Valuations are obviously a lot more appealing right now, and earnings growth is moving in the right direction.

This AI infrastructure thesis isn't looking backward.

Instead, it's looking forward, and investors are digesting the war uncertainties and informing us that this isn't enough to cause a bear market.

Plus, with the U.S. appearing not likely to force a ground invasion, which could make matters far worse and induce a prolonged conflict, we are moving closer to actual de-escalation.

And as a reminder, I also highlighted these glaring bottoming signals to my subscribers in my investing group (Ultimate Growth Investing) on April 1.

I reminded them how the market devoured the short sellers to end the month of March, and the bullish reversal signals were clearly established, encouraging them to take advantage while the rest looked away.

So, as semiconductor stocks and surrounding AI picks and shovel plays continue to blow us away, refreshing the power of the AI buildout thesis, you might want to have a more decisive plan to put capital to work sooner rather than later.

By the time the coast is clear, I guess we will all be waiting for you at the next high, yeah?

Adieus.

data.title: QDTE: 21% Yielding Weekly Cash Machine To Enhance Portfolio Yield
data.description:
Back in December 2025, I wrote an article on the Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (QDTE) suggesting investors to rotate out of the ETF before a larger correction takes place.

In my view, the market was ripe for a pullback. Obviously, I had no idea about the war in Iran but simply the combination of historically high valuations, three exceptional bull-years behind us, and increasing risks at geopolitical and domestic economy fronts made me very cautious about large-cap, AI-linked growth exposure.

With this baseline in mind going long QDTE would not have been the best move out there. The tariff-tantrum event serves a great example in which the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) experienced a notable correction in a short period of time.

This is how it ended for QDTE investors:

In this chart we can see the manifestation of the biggest drawback that zero-day-expiration covered call strategies embody which is that they introduce aggressive caps on the upside moves.

Here we can better see the issue (i.e., limited participation in a recovery process, especially if the rate of change is notable):

So, my concern going into 2026 was that we would see a major dip in pricey large-cap names such as Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), Tesla (TSLA) and Microsoft (MSFT), which would be followed by a significant bounce back - just as it always has been the case for the past several years (e.g., the Fed put, the Trump put, etc.). Hence, QDTE was not my preferred pick, to say the least.

However, some things have changed and, in my view, the conditions for considering an entry in QDTE have improved. Let me explain.

Thesis review

The key change since the moment when I released my previous piece on QDTE is the fact that the volatility levels have massively increased.

Just take a look at the chart below:

In the past week or so the VIX has decreased a bit, but it does not change the essence which is that the option contracts are much more expensive than at the very start of this year.

There is a plethora of drivers behind this dynamic such as the war in Iran, surging oil prices, increased fiscal spending, rising inflation, cracks in the private credit and BDC (BIZD) space, back-end-loaded interest rate cuts, supply chain disruptions etc.

All this creates a favorable ground for structurally higher volatility. Granted, there might be periods of contracting VIX but still it is hard to imagine how the "uncertainty premium" could disappear any time soon (e.g., even if the war ends tomorrow, we will have to live with the lagged effects from the crisis which will show up in the data months or quarters later).

Now, how does it translate to QDTE's investment case?

Since QDTE sells covered call options on the Nasdaq-100 (which has become subject to heightened volatility and uncertainty), each contract brings in more attractive option premiums.

For example, if the market (or the Nasdaq-100 in this case) trades sideways or gradually increases or decreases QDTE would be in a better position to deliver alpha-like performance.

However, we have to be very cognizant of the risk that I mentioned above - i.e., QDTE introducing a tangible opportunity cost in the case of a sudden bounce-back that would be followed after a leg-down move.

In this context, it might be worth taking a look at the following chart:

Here we can see how despite bleeding, the Nasdaq-100 had a sharper sell-off in late March (that was followed by a decent recovery). QDTE managed to slightly outperform the index. Interestingly, starting from February until the major pullback in March QDTE had built-up a meaningful alpha over the index.

I have two explanations for this:

  1. QDTE utilized a covered call strategy which entails selling daily option contracts that are slightly out-of-the-money which in the vehicle leaves some upside potential open.
  2. The ETF is actively managed which comes in handy in an environment like this when the odds for both sudden downside and a subsequent recovery are huge. Namely, the management can calibrate how far out-of-the-money covered call contracts are written which as we can see in the chart above have definitely kept some upside potential open.

However, the risk of experiencing an opportunity cost, while mitigated, is still inherent in QDTE. Each day QDTE writes covered call option contracts against QQQ which per definition caps part of the upside. The prospectus doesn't specify a ceiling for how far out-of-the-money QDTE can sell calls. However, I doubt we would be protected against underperformance if the QQQ rallies more than 5% in a single day (which is not a low probability even when the uncertainty is so high as it is now).

Finally, let me quickly elaborate on the dividend aspect.

The current dividend yield of QDTE stands at ~46%. Yet it would be wrong to fixate our expectations on this figure as it includes dividends which have been distributed in the past 12-month period. Going forward, QDTE will have (and already has) lower dividends than in the past.

The main reason is related to two larger special distributions which happened in December 2025: i.e., $1.7210 and $1.9151 per share (the weekly average payment up until then was around $0.21 per share). These two special distributions significantly reduced NAV which, in turn, has limited the amounts that we can get from new option contracts as the underlying notional base is smaller.

To get a more reliable and relevant distribution yield, I would suggest aggregating the most recent four weekly payments and then annualizing them.

This way we will arrive at yield of ~21% which is still very high and enough to move the needle for most income-oriented investors.

The bottom line

In my view, QDTE is a covered call ETF that should be seriously considered by investors who:

  • Don't have meaningful exposure to the AI world and thus would benefit from having such a position (through the diversification angle).
  • Want to integrate an AI or large-cap growth factor into their portfolios in a manner that wouldn't dilute their portfolio yields but, if possible, enhance them even further.
  • Think that the chances of a very deep plunge and a sharp recovery in the subsequent day are limited (this is the area where QDTE is the most vulnerable).

Given the higher-for-longer volatility environment and QDTE's active strategy on the OTM covered call writing front, I am rating this ETF a buy now.

data.title: In 2026's Wartime Markets, Risky Debt Outshines Treasuries
data.description:
Bonds are prized for offering stability in an asset‑allocation strategy, providing an offset to the higher risk in stocks, particularly during periods of market stress. But since the Iran war began, fixed‑income securities have had a rough ride as markets struggle to assess whether the conflict’s main threat is higher inflation, slower growth (if not recession), or some mix of both.

A broad review of the US bond market, using a set of ETF proxies, shows that some corners of fixed income are posting gains since the conflict began on Feb. 28. But the winners aren’t the usual suspects that you would expect to shine an energy shock and a Middle East war.

The leading performer so far since the fighting started—through yesterday’s close (Apr. 13)—is bank loans. The Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN) is up more than 2%, far ahead of the rest of the fixed‑income field. Before the war, few investors thinking about safe havens for a new Middle East conflict would have anticipated that BKLN’s portfolio would become a go‑to port in the storm, but here we are.

BKLN primarily holds floating‑rate senior secured loans—also known as leveraged loans—issued by US corporations (see Morningstar profile below). These loans sit high in the capital structure and typically carry below‑investment‑grade credit ratings. The fund’s relatively strong performance suggests that a preference for higher yield, by way of higher risk, has been a motivating factor for investors during the current turmoil.

A conventional junk‑bond ETF is in second place during the war‑driven rally in select slices of the bond market: the SPDR Bloomberg Short Term High Yield Bond ETF (SJNK) is up 0.6%. Just behind it is the iShares 0–5 Year TIPS Bond ETF (STIP), edging up 0.5%.

Meanwhile, medium‑ and longer‑term Treasuries are underwater so far during the war period. The biggest loser is long‑term government bonds (TLT), which have tumbled 3.8%.

One interpretation: inflation worries are dominating risk perceptions for Treasuries. By contrast, the view that government bonds would benefit from heightened anxiety about slower economic activity or recession seems to be in remission at the moment.

But this explanation isn’t fully satisfying. The Treasury market’s implied inflation forecast has remained relatively stable since the war’s start. The so‑called breakeven rate—the yield spread between nominal and inflation‑indexed Treasuries—hasn’t changed much in recent weeks and is still trading in the mid‑2% range.

If inflation isn’t weighing on Treasuries, what is? Fiscal risk may be a factor. As I’ve been discussing at TMC Research recently, the government’s budget deficit continues to flash warning signs. The high price tag for the war isn’t helping.

Is the bond market looking for new safe havens these days? Hard to know for sure, but the pivot into high‑risk bond securities doesn’t fit neatly into the standard playbook for periods when geopolitical risk spikes.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Alphabet: A Powerful Earnings Beat Likely Coming Again (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Previewing Alphabet's Upcoming Earnings Release

Last time I looked at Alphabet (GOOG), in July 2025, the stock was trading at ~$176/share, and I saw a diminishing margin of safety, whereas Microsoft (MSFT), OpenAI (OPENAI), and Anthropic (ANTHRO) seemed to pose some vulnerabilities to its future business development at the time. I rated GOOG as a "Hold", and now I understand that I clearly underestimated Google's AI offering and their balanced business approach of both hardware and software products development.

The company is set to report fiscal Q1 2026 in about two weeks, and based on the data we have on hand, Google is very likely to beat this time, just as it has in recent quarters. More importantly, I see management guiding strongly for calendar 2026, which should lead to above-consensus forward projections. The incoming 'depreciation wave' versus top-line acceleration will be in focus on the earnings call, but given existing operating leverage and GCP momentum, I don't see any serious threats this time. Based on all this, I'm upgrading my previous "Hold" to "Buy" ahead of the earnings print, expecting a continuation of the post-earnings rally as valuation multiples have already shrunk quite meaningfully.

Reasoning For The Upgrade Ahead Of Q1

The reason for my skepticism about Google was about at least partial cannibalization of their high-margin Search business with Gemini (and, of course, other LLMs). The LLMs market was dominated by OpenAI at the time, and I feared that the loss of traffic on the Search side would damage Google's moat (and hence there would be no reason for a higher valuation multiple expectation). It's now clear that I was wrong - OpenAI's market share is dropping, and while Anthropic is the ultimate winner in the enterprise niche (for now, at least), Gemini models are gaining market share in the consumer niche as well. According to AI Multipe data, OpenAI's market share contracted from ~72.5% as of October 2025 to 60.5% as of February 2026 (and I guess right now it's even lower). Gemini's share skyrocketed from less than 14% to almost 24% for the same period.

The "loss of Search" as a fear hasn't materialized - Google's business focus has shifted, but it looks to be for the better. The management commented on the previous call that with the Gemini app scaling to over 700 million MAU, AI Mode queries in Search have proven to be 3x longer than traditional searches:

Queries in AI Mode are 3x longer than traditional searches. We are also seeing sessions become more conversational with a significant portion of queries in AI Mode now leading to a follow-up question.

Source: GOOG's Q4 earningns call

It means that the whole user engagement has become deeper, and hence it now has a better monetization potential for Google in the future periods.

Google is spending tens of billions of dollars on AI infrastructure, but the returns on those investments are actually quite solid so far (they're at least justified). Google Cloud (GCP) revenues accelerated to $17.7 billion in the past quarter (+48% YoY), and this segment's EBIT doubled for the period. The backlog surged by 55% QoQ and amounted to $240 billion, and it's going to be supported by the CAPEX they're spending at the moment. For FY2026, the management guided for $175-185 billion in CAPEX, which is going to be a doubling from FY2025, but the backlog justifies it all, in my view. Most of the invested money will flow to custom silicon (specifically the new Ironwood, TPU7x, chips) that lower energy needs - the ROIC on AI workflows Google will be selling in the next few years, once the hardware optimizations kick in, are going to be much higher than most skeptics might be expecting at the moment. Following the rollout of the more efficient Axion CPUs, Alphabet has already reduced the serving unit cost of Gemini by ~78% over 2025, and it looks to be just the beginning, with more efficiencies from their custom silicon on the horizon.

Now, let me jump to the earnings preview part of my GOOG update and see what's expected. The consensus is waiting for Q1 revenues of $106.89 billion (+18.46% YoY), according to Seeking Alpha, with an adjusted EPS consensus figure set at $2.62 (-6.81% YoY).

When the company reported last time, we saw a double-beating, but the stock price closed almost 3% on the release day because the market feared the earnings regarding depreciation expenses, which were projected to increase meaningfully in Q1.

In terms of expenses, as we've discussed on previous calls, the significant increase in our investments in technical infrastructure will continue to put pressure on the P&L in the form of higher depreciation expense and related data centers operations costs such as energy. In 2025, depreciation increased by nearly $6 billion or 38% from $15.3 billion in 2024 to $21.1 billion in 2025. Given the increase in our CapEx investments in recent years, we expect the growth rate in 2026 depreciation to accelerate in Q1 and meaningfully increase for the full year. We're also planning to continue hiring in key investment areas such as AI and cloud.

Source: GOOG's Q4 earningns call

Keeping in mind FY2026 CAPEX plans of up to $185 billion, I think depreciation could easily surpass $30 billion for the fiscal year 2026, and it's effectively pulling billions in non-cash expenses directly through the firm's P&L. The top line keeps expanding, which is good, but the bottom line remains under pressure for now. At least because of CAPEX, it's not at the level it would have been without it.

Google will have to show its GCP's superior expansion capabilities again, preferably against the backdrop of lower Gemini serving costs - in this case, the operating leverage would be de-risked, and Google's margins will hold steady near 31.6% despite the accounting drag. I expect that some of the $240 billion backlog will convert to revenues, providing a nice buffer for yet another top-line beating. Plus, GCP is very unlikely to lose momentum, driven by the previously mentioned backlog conversion. Also, based on BofA's note to their clients (proprietary source, April 9, 2026), Google's Search traffic is up materially on a YoY basis, and Gemini is still eating away at ChatGPT's market share (for common use purposes, I guess, while Anthropic's Claude is killing it for the enterprises).

BofA analysts added:

For Gemini, traffic and user growth trends remain strong whileGoogle traffic growth was stable y/y, suggesting Claude's traction was incremental or at expense of AI competitors. We continue to see an opportunity for AI integration in Google Search to drive revenue upside through improved intent understanding and better monetization of zero-click queries. Maintain Buy.

What I see as another bullish catalyst that might come right from the upcoming earnings call is the management's wider discussion of the TurboQuant algorithm revealed last month. TurboQuant sent most memory chip manufacturers' stocks to the downside for some time, and while I don't think it's a really huge risk for stocks like SK Hynix or Micron (MU) - see my latest take here - I do believe that for Google, it might be another cost optimization tool that should help them drive up the margins (or make them more stable while they keep spending billions on AI infrastructure projects).

What I also like about Google as it's approaching its Q1 earnings print is the fact that it's trading below its 10-year average P/E ratio on a forward basis, per YCharts data:

Assuming that the majority of CAPEX is spent in 2025-2027, and the past investments start to pay off big way, the margins should pick up eventually and let the EPS soar. Based on the current consensus, which is historically quite pessimistic in Google's case, the firm's EPS should be rising at a 5-year CAGR of ~21.1%. Realistically, Google's EPS should triple in the next 5 years, and the stock is priced at less than 24x FY2027 earnings:

If we assume that GOOG trades at 30x FY2027, with no premium added to the bottom line this time, the stock's fair value arrives at $402.6, which is 26%+ above today's stock price.

Final Words

Of course, there are some clear risks to my today's bullish upgrade on Google. First, given the fact that AI Overviews now appear on 48% of all search queries, and it can summarize any data perfectly, the click-through rates (CTR) fall sharply, so the cannibalization of traffic that I feared last time is actually happening in some form.
